region_of_origin: India (served widely in Singapore as Indian street food)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Aloo Channa Chaat; closest defensible anchor is 'chickpea dish' (FOODON_03311737). A more specific term for chaat-style mixed legume-potato dishes does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chickpea-based dish (mixed legume and vegetable preparation)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chickpea) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 term exists for chaat-style mixed potato-chickpea dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Aloo Channa Chaat or equivalent chaat-style dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Aloo Channa Chaat is approximately 250 g (1 bowl), as commonly portioned in Indian street food and restaurant settings in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central — composite estimate for chickpea-potato chaat
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Aloo Channa Chaat as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for boiled chickpeas, boiled potatoes, and common chaat condiments, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for chickpeas (FDC ID 173757) and potatoes (FDC ID 170026). The input record's per_100g values (energy 128 kcal, protein 4 g, fat 3.2 g, carbohydrate 20 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Sodium at 260 mg/100g reflects chaat masala and black salt additions. Values are classified as medium confidence due to recipe variability.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — chickpeas (boiled) and potato composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Channa Chaat as a composite dish. GI estimated as a weighted average of boiled chickpeas (GI ~28, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled potato (GI ~78, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted approximately 50:50 by carbohydrate contribution, yielding a composite GI of approximately 41 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (41 × 50 g carbohydrate in 250 g serving) / 100 = 20.5. The chickpea component substantially lowers the overall GI relative to potato alone. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 260 mg/100g from chaat masala and black salt; potassium ~340 mg/100g partially offsets
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced chaat masala and black salt, avoid or limit sev, and choose a low-sodium tamarind chutney, as sodium at 260 mg/100g (650 mg per 250 g serving) is borderline for a single snack; the potassium from chickpeas and potatoes (~850 mg per serving) provides a meaningful Na:K offset but does not fully neutralise the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite GI ~41 (low) driven by chickpeas; carbohydrate 20 g/100g with 3.2 g dietary fibre moderating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Aloo Channa Chaat in moderate portions (150–200 g) as the chickpea component lowers the composite GI to approximately 41 and dietary fibre at 3.2 g/100g slows glucose absorption; however, portion control is essential as the potato component is high-GI and tamarind chutney may contain added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.0 g/100g is low; sodium 260 mg/100g and trans fat 0.04 g/100g (from sev) are mild concerns; chickpea fibre is cardioprotective
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Aloo Channa Chaat occasionally as saturated fat is low (1.0 g/100g) and chickpea dietary fibre (3.2 g/100g) supports LDL reduction; however, sodium at 260 mg/100g and the presence of trans fat from fried sev (0.04 g/100g) warrant requesting omission of sev and reduced seasoning to minimise cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, green chilli) may trigger joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (solanine) should avoid or significantly modify Aloo Channa Chaat by requesting substitution of potato and tomato with non-nightshade vegetables, and omitting sev which generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) through deep-frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Channa Chaat is a popular Indian street food snack widely consumed in India and among the Indian diaspora in Singapore. It is prepared by combining boiled potatoes (aloo) and boiled chickpeas (channa/chana) with a medley of chutneys — typically tamarind (imli) chutney and green (mint-coriander) chutney — along with finely chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, fresh coriander, lemon juice, chaat masala, cumin powder, and black salt (kala namak). Optional crunchy toppings include sev (thin fried chickpea-flour noodles) and papdi (fried wheat crackers), and yogurt is sometimes added for creaminess. The dish is served cold or at room temperature, assembled just before eating to preserve texture. The flavour profile is characteristically tangy, spicy, and slightly sweet, with a contrast of soft potato and chickpea against crunchy sev. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 128 kcal, 4 g protein, 3.2 g fat, 20 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 260 mg sodium. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 41 (low) due to the chickpea component, making it more glycaemically favourable than potato-only dishes. Chickpeas contribute plant protein, soluble fibre, iron (1.2 mg/100g), and calcium (32 mg/100g). Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires sodium management for hypertension and portion control for Type 2 Diabetes due to the potato content. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable in standard preparation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central component data for boiled chickpeas and boiled potatoes; no single approved-source entry exists for Aloo Channa Chaat as a composite dish. GI is an analogous weighted estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for chickpeas and potato. Recipe variability (sev, yogurt, chutney quantities) introduces uncertainty. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite chaat dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level data would require recipe-level disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this assembled dish is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish is not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; individual component values exist but composite dish-level data is unavailable. potassium_mg value of 340 mg/100g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for chickpeas and potatoes and is not directly sourced from a single approved-source composite entry. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g/100g reflects trace trans fat from sev (fried chickpea-flour noodles) if included; this value is an estimate. The input record's ethnic_tags ('Biryani', 'Butter Chicken', 'Naan') were removed as they are irrelevant to Aloo Channa Chaat and appear to be erroneous metadata. country_of_origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'India' as Aloo Channa Chaat is an Indian-origin dish; it is served in Singapore but did not originate there.
